items.title: EVs and the fuel levy question
items.summary: South Africa's roads are funded largely through the fuel levy — a tax built into the price of every litre of petrol or diesel sold. The more fuel you buy, the more you contribute. It…
items.content_html:
South Africa's roads are funded largely through the fuel levy — a tax built into the price of every litre of petrol or diesel sold. The more fuel you buy, the more you contribute. It is a simple system, and for decades it has worked reasonably well.

But battery electric vehicles (BEVs) do not use these fuels. They charge from the grid, and in doing so, they avoid the fuel levy entirely. As more South Africans switch to BEVs, the amount collected through the fuel levy will shrink — even though the roads those BEVs drive on still need to be built, maintained and repaired.

This is the funding gap that transport planners and economists are increasingly worried about.

Fuel levy figures tell the story

The general fuel levy has been rising steadily.

From June 2025, it stood at R4.01 per litre for petrol and R3.85 per litre for diesel. By April 2026, those figures had increased further to R4.29 per litre for petrol and R4.16 cents per litre for diesel. The Road Accident Fund levy, which funds compensation for road crash victims, also rose to R2.25 per litre.

A petrol car filling up a 50-litre tank is therefore contributing well over R300 in levies alone, every single time. An EV owner filling the same journey pays none of that.

Why a road-use charge makes sense

The idea behind a road-use charge is straightforward.

Instead of taxing the fuel a vehicle burns, the government would charge drivers based on how far they travel. The more kilometres you drive, the more you pay. It does not matter whether your vehicle runs on petrol, diesel, hydrogen or electricity — if you use the road, you contribute to its upkeep.

This also addresses a quieter problem that already exists. A fuel-efficient petrol car consuming six litres per 100 km pays far less in fuel levy per kilometre than an older car consuming 11 litres per 100 km. Both vehicles cause similar wear on the road surface, yet one contributes significantly less to road funding. A per-kilometre charge would treat them the same.

How it could work in practice

There are a few ways a road-use charge could be collected.

The simplest approach would be an odometer reading taken at licensing or roadworthy time each year*. You declare your kilometres, and a charge is calculated accordingly. It is easy to administer and raises few privacy concerns.

A more sophisticated approach would use telematics or GPS tracking. This would allow charges to vary depending on the type of road used, the time of day, traffic conditions and even the weight of the vehicle. A Western Cape pilot concept has already explored this idea, using route-based pricing to show how detailed such a system could become.

The third option is a flat annual fee added to vehicle licence renewal. This is the easiest to implement, but it treats a driver covering 5 000 km a year the same as one covering 50 000 km — which many would consider unfair.

What this means for EV owners

Lower running costs remain one of the strongest arguments for going electric, and that is unlikely to change. A road-use charge, if introduced, would add a new line to the ownership cost — but it would apply to all vehicles, not just BEVs.

The important thing for BEV owners is to follow the debate closely, keep track of annual mileage, and engage when draft proposals are published. The details — how privacy is protected, how rural drivers are treated, how the system is administered — will matter enormously.

How South Africa funds its roads in an electric future is one that government, industry and the public will need to answer together.

*A few issues are worth noting. Firstly, South Africa currently does not require annual roadworthy testing. Secondly, using odometer readings will likely increase the likelihood of "mileage manipulation", especially on older vehicles - Ed.

items.title: BYD raises the stakes with ATTO 8 launch
items.summary: Chinese new-energy vehicle giant brings seven-seat plug-in hybrid to market at prices starting from just over R1m BYD has officially launched the ATTO 8 in South Africa, bringing a large seven-seat plug-in hybrid SUV to…
items.content_html:
Chinese new-energy vehicle giant brings seven-seat plug-in hybrid to market at prices starting from just over R1m

BYD has officially launched the ATTO 8 in South Africa, bringing a large seven-seat plug-in hybrid SUV to a market that is showing growing appetite for electrified family transport.

The ATTO 8, which is sold under the Tang L and Sealion 8 nameplates in other markets, arrives in two variants. The Premium edition is priced at R1 059 900, while the Performance AWD edition carries a sticker price of R1 259 900. 

Two powertrains, one platform

At the heart of both variants is BYD's fifth-generation DM Super Hybrid Technology, which pairs a 1.5-litre turbocharged four-cylinder petrol engine producing 110 kW with one or two electric motors, depending on the specification chosen. 

The Premium model uses a single front-mounted electric motor for a combined system output of 205 kW, drawing on a 19 kWh lithium-iron-phosphate Blade Battery for a claimed electric-only range of 75 km and a total combined range of 892 km.

The Performance AWD variant adds a second electric motor on the rear axle, lifting combined output to 359 kW and 675 Nm. Its larger 35.6 kWh battery delivers a claimed 130 km of pure electric driving, with a combined range of 844 km. The sprint from standstill to 100 km/h takes a claimed 4.9 seconds in this configuration.

Measuring 5 040 mm in length with a 2 950 mm wheelbase, the ATTO 8 is a full-size crossover that positions itself alongside established three-row rivals such as the Audi Q7 and Volvo EX90 in terms of sheer footprint.

Space, specification and a serious warranty

Standard equipment on the Premium model includes a 15.6-inch rotating infotainment display, a 10.25-inch digital instrument cluster, 20-inch alloy wheels, a 10-speaker audio system, wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto, and powered, heated and ventilated front seats. 

The Performance model upgrades to 21-inch wheels, a head-up display, adaptive suspension, a 21-speaker sound system, and heated and ventilated second-row seats with massage functionality. Bamboo-wood trim, leather upholstery and a 360-degree camera system feature across the range.

Boot capacity with all three rows in use stands at 270 litres, expanding to 960 litres with the third row folded and 1 960 litres with both rear rows stowed.

Steve Chang, managing director of BYD Auto South Africa, said the launch represented a deepening of the brand's commitment to the local market. "The ATTO 8 combines innovation, space and performance in a way that feels both premium and accessible, whether you are navigating the city or travelling with family over long distances," he said.

Commercial director Mark Handley added that the model was intended to broaden BYD's customer base beyond early adopters. "For us, the ATTO 8 is more than just a new model. It is an opportunity to grow the BYD customer base in South Africa, to reach new households and continue building confidence in our brand," he said.

Each ATTO 8 comes with a five-year/100 000 km vehicle warranty, an eight-year/200 000 km battery warranty, and a five-year/100 000 km maintenance plan.

The launch follows a period of rapid expansion for BYD in South Africa, where the brand has steadily widened its model range from entry-level offerings such as the Dolphin Surf to vehicles that compete squarely in the upper-mid segment. 

The ATTO 8 represents the most direct challenge yet to established European and Japanese three-row SUVs in the country's premium family vehicle space.

items.title: NEV sales grow as EV numbers slip
items.summary: South Africa's New Energy Vehicle (NEV) market, which includes hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs) alongside battery electric vehicles (BEVs), grew 7.1% to 16,716 units in 2025. This follows a doubling of NEV sales…
items.content_html:
South Africa's New Energy Vehicle (NEV) market, which includes hybrid and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs) alongside battery electric vehicles (BEVs), grew 7.1% to 16,716 units in 2025. 

This follows a doubling of NEV sales in 2024, which industry body naamsa | the Automotive Business Council described as "early-stage demand momentum."

BEV sales, however, told a more sobering story. 

Pure electric vehicle registrations declined from 1,257 units in 2024 to 1,088 units in 2025, a retreat that industry participants attributed to a combination of high purchase prices, inadequate charging infrastructure, and punishing import duties.

A Taxing Situation

Tshetlhe Litheko, Chief Policy Officer at naamsa | the Automotive Business Council, noted that South Africa's EV import duty of approximately 25%, compounded by VAT and an ad valorem luxury tax, is in sharp contrast to the 10% levied by the European Union and the 2.5% base rate applied in the United States. 

China, meanwhile, has moved to eliminate import duties on EVs for many trading partners, while simultaneously removing domestic purchase taxes to accelerate adoption.

“South Africa holds a proactive NEV and EV transition agenda, with a 150% investment incentive effective from March 2026," Litheko said, pointing to the NEV roadmap milestone that became operative last month.

Furthermore, the South African Automotive Masterplan (SAAM 2035) wants 60% of new vehicle sales in SA by 2035 to be NEVs.

Infrastructure and affordability the central challenges

Despite policy progress, the gap between aspiration and reality remains wide. 

South Africa has fewer than 400 public charging stations compared with approximately 4,800 petrol stations. Grid instability further complicates reliable charging access, though solar-powered charging solutions are beginning to emerge as a practical workaround.

Affordability, though, remains the sharpest constraint. 

Most EVs on the South African market are priced above R600 000, yet 74% of prospective buyers are targeting vehicles below R400 000. That mismatch will require both policy intervention and a broader range of affordable EV models to resolve.

The aftermarket opportunity

“The aftermarket opportunity in EVs is substantial,” says Anton Fiets, Executive Manager for Industry Development at the Automotive Industry Development Centre (AIDC). 

The AIDC's Manufacturing Centre of Excellence, currently in its first phase, incorporates EV simulation and high-voltage training systems alongside mechatronics and advanced assembly workstations.

Fiets points to battery diagnostics, replacement and recycling services, software and telematics support, and specialist EV parts supply as areas where South African businesses could build competitive positions, drawing on lessons from China, Europe, and the United States.

Manufacturing and logistics pressure increases

Ongoing conflict in the Middle East has pushed oil above $110 per barrel, raising manufacturing costs and logistics pressures. 

US tariffs, rapid technological change, and intensifying competition from Asian EV imports are reshaping the OEM landscape, while the growth of wholly imported completely knocked-down (WICKD) models threatens to reduce localisation levels.

KZN's business confidence reached a historic high of 60 points in the fourth quarter of 2025, according to the Bureau for Economic Research, making it South Africa's most confident business hub. 

Whether that confidence translates into the investment needed to accelerate the NEV transition locally will depend, in large part, on whether government and industry can close the gap between policy ambition and on-the-ground infrastructure reality.

items.title: Cape Town to get SA's first locally built electric buses
items.summary: Cape Town is preparing to take delivery of its first electric bus in August, the start of a phased rollout that will bring 30 new vehicles into the MyCiTi fleet by mid-2027. The 12-metre Volvo…
items.content_html:
Cape Town is preparing to take delivery of its first electric bus in August, the start of a phased rollout that will bring 30 new vehicles into the MyCiTi fleet by mid-2027.

The 12-metre Volvo BZRLE buses will be the first electric buses in South Africa to have their bodies manufactured locally. The bodywork will be produced in Johannesburg, giving the project an added dimension as a boost to domestic industry.

The City of Cape Town's Urban Mobility Directorate will receive the first bus in August, followed by 13 more before the end of the year. The remaining vehicles will arrive by June 2027.

Subject to a successful testing period, passenger services are expected to begin on 1 July 2027, on routes linking Mitchells Plain and Khayelitsha with Wynberg, Claremont and the Cape Town CBD.

The new buses will serve MyCiTi's metro south-east network, including routes forming part of the Phase 2A expansion. Two new depots built to house the fleet were completed in October last year at a cost of R430 million.

Each facility spans 7.5 hectares and includes underground charging infrastructure, mechanical workshops and capacity for up to 250 buses as the fleet grows. Rob Quintas, the mayoral committee member for urban mobility, said the timing of the rollout was well suited to the current environment.

"The delivery of the electric bus fleet cannot be more timeous, given the volatility of the energy markets and uncertain geopolitical environment," he said. "Apart from lowering our carbon emissions, an electric bus fleet could offer multiple other benefits, especially as far as maintenance and operational costs are concerned."

International experience points to operating cost reductions of up to 70% for electric bus fleets, though the city will conduct its own research to determine what savings are achievable locally. Before the buses enter regular service, the city will run a 12-month testing programme in partnership with the University of Cape Town.

The research, funded jointly by the city and the Urban Electric Mobility Initiative, will assess battery performance across different route profiles, the effect of passenger loads and weather conditions on energy consumption, and charging times. The findings will inform driver training, maintenance planning and the broader rollout scheduled for 2027.

The city has indicated that acquiring additional electric buses remains a priority. Further expansion will depend on funding from national government. Cape Town's MyCiTi network currently operates 347 buses across 39 routes, carrying around 2.3 million passengers a month.

The electric fleet represents the most significant change to the service since its Phase 2A expansion began, and positions the city as the first in South Africa to operate locally built electric buses at scale.

items.title: Public Charging in South Africa: Practical Planning for the Road
items.summary: Public charging is the part of EV ownership that gets the most attention. It’s the part you see on the news and the part that looks the most familiar. But using a public charger in…
items.content_html:
Public charging is the part of EV ownership that gets the most attention. It’s the part you see on the news and the part that looks the most familiar.

But using a public charger in South Africa is not quite the same as pulling up to a petrol pump. It requires a different way of thinking.

The networks are growing, and the technology is improving, but it is still a system in progress. If you understand how it works now, you can avoid the frustration that many new owners face.

Charging speed is a maximum, not a promise

One of the first things you will notice is that public chargers are labelled with a speed, like 60kW, 80kW or 150kW. It is easy to assume that if you plug into a 150kW charger, your car will always charge at that speed. In reality, it is more complicated.

The speed you get depends on several factors:

  • Your car’s limit: Every EV has a maximum speed it can accept. If your car is limited to 100kW, it will never charge faster than that, even on a 300kW charger.
  • Battery fullness: Charging is fastest when the battery is low. As the battery fills up, it slows down to protect the cells.
  • Temperature: If the battery is excessively hot or cold, the charging speed will drop.
  • Shared power: Many public units have two plugs. If someone else is charging at the same time, the unit often splits its power between the two of you.

Think of the number on the charger as a ceiling, not a guarantee.

Managing the "App Problem"

In South Africa, public charging is managed by several different companies. Most of them have their own apps, payment systems and membership cards. Currently, there is no single app that covers every charger in the country. This means preparation is key.

Before you set off on a trip, you should:

  • Identify which networks are on your route (such as GridCars, Rubicon or others).
  • Download the relevant apps and register your details.
  • Load some credit into the digital wallets on those apps.

Trying to download an app and enter credit card details while standing at a charger in a remote area is a situation you want to avoid. Five minutes of setup at home saves half an hour of stress on the road.

Why the "Plan B" is essential

South Africa presents unique challenges for any electrical network. Infrastructure can be affected by weather, technical faults, or even simple connectivity issues. A charger that shows as "Available" on a map might be offline when you arrive. Or, someone else might be using it, and they could be there for another 40 minutes.

This is why experienced EV owners in South Africa always have a backup plan.

If you are planning to stop at a specific charger, always know where the next nearest one is. Never arrive at a charger with so little battery that you couldn't reach your backup option if you had to. A 15% to 20% safety margin is a sensible rule for long-distance travel here.

The practicalities of a charging stop

What does a real charging stop look like? Public DC chargers use large, heavy cables that are permanently attached to the unit. You park, plug in, and then use your app or an RFID card to start the session.

While the car charges, you generally leave it. This is why chargers are located at malls, convenience pulls, or farm stalls. You go for a coffee, use the bathroom, or stretch your legs. By the time you are ready to leave, the car usually is too.

Most owners find that they don't "wait" for the car to charge. Instead, the car charges while they take a necessary break from driving.

A note on load shedding and reliability

One of the most common questions in South Africa is what happens during load shedding. Many public charging sites at major malls or highway stops have backup power, such as large generators or solar-and-battery systems. However, not all of them do.

Modern charging apps often show the live status of a charger. If a site is down due to a power cut, it will usually show as offline in the app. Checking the app ten minutes before you arrive can save you a wasted detour.

Improving your route planning

For daily driving in the city, you will likely never need a public charger. For long trips, a little digital help goes a long way.

There are tools and websites that help you plan which chargers to use based on your specific car and the route you are taking. They can even predict how much battery you will have left at each stop.

Using these tools removes the guesswork. You stop being a pioneer and start being someone who is just following a map.

The bottom line on public charging

Public charging in South Africa is good enough for most major routes, but it is not yet perfect. It requires you to be a little more organised than you would be in a petrol car. You need the right apps, a little bit of credit, and a clear idea of where your backup stops are.

Once you have those basics sorted, the "anxiety" of charging mostly disappears. It just becomes another part of the journey.

In the next article, we will move from the road to the driveway. We’ll look at what you actually need to do in the first week of owning your EV to make sure everything is set up correctly for the long term.

items.title: Home Charging Key For Electric Taxis
items.summary: A new study published in Nature Sustainability suggests that electrifying Cape Town's minibus taxi fleet is possible. But only if the right charging infrastructure is put in place. Minibus taxis are the lifeblood of public…
items.content_html:
A new study published in Nature Sustainability suggests that electrifying Cape Town's minibus taxi fleet is possible. But only if the right charging infrastructure is put in place.

Minibus taxis are the lifeblood of public transport in South Africa. More than 300,000 of them carry around 11 million passengers every day. In Cape Town alone, taxis account for 67% of all public transport trips, serving roughly 830,000 passengers daily.

Most of these passengers live in historically marginalised communities, far from economic centres, and depend on taxis as their only realistic transport option.

Researchers from Stellenbosch University used tracking data from 671 minibus taxis participating in the Western Cape Government's BlueDot programme to assess whether electric vehicles could realistically replace the current petrol and diesel fleet. 

The BlueDot programme fitted taxis with GPS trackers to monitor driving behaviour and reward safe driving. This gave the researchers an unusually detailed, real-world dataset to work with (covering around 12,000 vehicle-days of operation).

Charging access is the deciding factor

The study found that electrification is technically viable, but the results depend heavily on where and when taxis can charge. When taxis had access to both depot charging and home charging, between 92% and 97% of daily operations were successful. When taxis could only charge at depots, that figure dropped to between 61% and 83%.

Interestingly, fast charging made very little difference. Increasing charger power from 22 kilowatts to 50 kilowatts improved viability by only one to five percentage points. Expanding access to more charging locations had a far greater impact than increasing charging speed.

A problem for the electricity grid

Electrifying the taxi fleet would add significant new demand to the electricity grid. The study found that unmanaged charging tends to create sharp peaks in the early morning and evening, times when the grid is already under strain. 

Home charging, in particular, generates an evening peak around 8pm, which coincides with the surge in residential electricity use from households switching on appliances and water heaters.

The researchers suggest that managed charging strategies could help. Time-of-use tariffs, centralised scheduling, and solar-aligned charging — where taxis charge during the middle of the day when solar generation is highest — could all help to smooth demand and reduce pressure on the grid.

Air quality improves, but carbon savings are limited (for now)

One of the clearest benefits of electric taxis is cleaner air. Electric vehicles produce no exhaust emissions, which means a significant reduction in fine particle pollution (PM2.5). This matters enormously for low-income communities, which are both the most reliant on taxis and the most exposed to poor air quality.

South Africa's carbon picture is more complicated. 

The country's electricity grid is still heavily dependent on coal, which means that electric taxis currently produce roughly the same amount of carbon dioxide as petrol vehicles. Diesel vehicles actually produce less in this comparison. As South Africa's grid becomes cleaner over time, the carbon benefits of electric taxis are expected to grow.

Equity must be at the centre of planning

The study highlights a serious equity concern. Drivers who live in informal settlements or areas with unreliable electricity access are less able to charge at home, which makes electric operation much harder for them.

The researchers recommend that authorities prioritise shared community charging hubs in townships and underserved areas, rather than relying solely on home charging.

The research was conducted by M.J. Booysen, B.G. Pretorius and J.T. Sello of Stellenbosch University and was published in Nature Sustainability in April 2026. The full paper is available at https://doi.org/10.1038/s41893-026-01808-9. Licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0.

items.title: SA’s EV Industry Has a Carbon Problem
items.summary: South Africa's electric vehicle sector is growing. New models are arriving, charging networks are expanding, and government is slowly warming to the idea of a cleaner transport future. But there is a problem that very…
items.content_html:
South Africa's electric vehicle sector is growing. New models are arriving, charging networks are expanding, and government is slowly warming to the idea of a cleaner transport future. 

But there is a problem that very few people in the industry are talking about openly. It has nothing to do with range anxiety or charging infrastructure. It has to do with the electricity that powers the factories making these vehicles, and a European trade rule that is already in force.

What is CBAM?

The EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism — known as CBAM — is a carbon tariff on goods imported into Europe. 

It targets products made using carbon-intensive energy. The idea is straightforward. If a factory in South Africa uses dirty, coal-fired electricity to manufacture goods, and those goods are then sold into the European Union, the buyer in Europe pays a carbon levy on top of the normal import price.

CBAM entered its transitional phase in October 2023. Full enforcement began in January 2026. 

Why this matters for South Africa's EV sector

South Africa's automotive industry is one of the country's most important export sectors. It supplies vehicles and components to markets around the world, including Europe. 

As the industry shifts towards electric vehicles, both in terms of what it assembles locally and what it exports, the carbon intensity of the electricity used in production becomes a direct business cost.

South Africa generates roughly 80% of its electricity from coal. That makes it one of the most carbon-intensive grids in the world. A vehicle or component assembled here, using grid electricity, carries a significant carbon footprint before it even leaves the factory floor.

Under CBAM, European importers of those goods will need to account for that carbon. They will pay for it. And in a competitive global market, that cost will eventually find its way back to South African manufacturers in the form of lower margins, lost contracts, or pressure to clean up their supply chains.

The electricity reform connection

This is where South Africa's ongoing electricity reform becomes directly relevant to the EV industry, and why a research report published in early 2026 by the South Africa Electricity Traders Association (SAETA) and research firm Krutham deserves attention from anyone working in this space.

The report, titled Policy to Power, makes the case that electricity market reform is not solely about keeping the lights on. It is also about decarbonisation, competitiveness, and reducing South Africa's exposure to exactly these kinds of carbon trade measures.

The report states clearly that market reform "underpins decarbonisation by enabling rapid investment in renewables, supporting corporate decarbonisation and reducing exposure to carbon border measures such as the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism."

In other words, the faster South Africa reforms its electricity market and opens it up to renewable energy, the faster manufacturers can access cleaner power, and the lower their CBAM exposure becomes.

The wheeling opportunity

One of the most practical tools available right now is electricity wheeling. This allows a business to buy renewable energy directly from an independent power producer and have it delivered across the national grid to their facility. 

Electricity traders, a relatively new category of licensed market participant in South Africa, play a central role here. They aggregate supply from multiple renewable generators and match it with business customers, including manufacturers. 

The SAETA report notes that traders already have a pipeline of 18 gigawatts of renewable energy projects in development. That is a significant pool of clean power waiting to reach businesses that need it.

For an EV manufacturer or component supplier looking to reduce its carbon footprint ahead of European export deadlines, this is not a theoretical option. It is available today, in metros and areas where wheeling frameworks are in place.

The catch

The problem is that access to wheeling is uneven. 

Only 10 of South Africa's 257 municipalities have adopted wheeling policies. That means a factory located in the wrong municipality simply cannot access green power through this route, regardless of how much renewable energy is available on the grid.

The SAETA report identifies this as one of the key blockages holding back electricity reform. Municipal distribution reform is listed as one of its ten priority actions. Until more municipalities open their networks to wheeling, the CBAM risk for manufacturers in those areas remains unaddressed.

What energy-intensive EV firms should be doing

The CBAM is not going away. If anything, the EU is likely to expand its scope over time. For South African businesses exporting to Europe, or hoping to, the core issue is how quickly they can adapt.

A few practical starting points:

  • Understand your carbon exposure. Map the electricity consumption in your production process and calculate the carbon intensity based on the South African grid average. This is the number that will determine your CBAM liability.
  • Explore wheeling options. If your facility is in a municipality with a wheeling policy, speak to a licensed electricity trader about accessing renewable power. The cost savings and carbon benefits can be significant.
  • Engage with the reform process. Industry bodies, including those representing the automotive and EV sectors, should be actively engaging with NERSA and the Department of Electricity and Energy on trading rules, wheeling frameworks, and the pace of electricity market reform. 

The bigger picture

South Africa has a narrow window to get this right. 

The SAETA report warns that load shedding could return from 2029 as coal plants retire and demand rises. At the same time, the pressure from CBAM and South Africa's own carbon tax is building. The two forces are converging on the same point: the electricity grid needs to get cleaner, faster.

The risk for the EV industry is that slow reform leaves manufacturers exposed to rising carbon costs on their European exports. The opportunity is that businesses which move early, securing green power through wheeling and traders, will have a genuine competitive advantage over those that wait.

Source: Policy to Power — Ten Actions to Deliver Green, Accessible and Secure Electricity, SAETA/Krutham (2026)

items.title: Where South Africans Charge Their EVs
items.summary: There's a common assumption about EV ownership. Most people imagine pulling up to a public charger, plugging in, and waiting. Like a petrol station, but slower. That picture is not wrong exactly. It just isn't…
items.content_html:
There's a common assumption about EV ownership. Most people imagine pulling up to a public charger, plugging in, and waiting. Like a petrol station, but slower. That picture is not wrong exactly. It just isn't the full story.

In practice, most EV owners in South Africa charge in far quieter, more ordinary moments. And understanding where that happens changes how you think about the whole ownership experience.

Home charging: where it usually starts

For most owners, home is where the majority of charging happens.

The reason is simple. The car is already there. It sits overnight, sometimes for eight hours or more. That time is useful. You don't need to go anywhere. You don't need to wait. You plug in before bed and wake up to a full battery.

This is what makes EV ownership feel easy for people whose home setup supports it. Charging stops being an activity. It becomes something that just happens.

What home charging requires

You don't need anything complicated to start charging at home. A standard wall socket can work, particularly if your daily mileage is low and the car has a long time to charge overnight. It is slow, but it gets the job done in many situations.

A wallbox is a step up. It is a dedicated charging unit, usually mounted on a wall in your garage or parking area. It charges faster than a standard socket and is generally the most practical long-term solution for home use.

A certified electrician needs to install it. Your car may come with one included, or with a voucher towards installation. It is worth checking before you buy.

Questions worth asking about your home setup

Before assuming home charging will work for you, a few things are worth thinking through. Where does your car park at home? Is there a power source nearby? Will a cable reach comfortably? Is the parking area covered or exposed to weather?

If you live in an estate or a complex, there may be rules about what you can install. Some body corporates are straightforward about this. Others require applications, approvals, or specific equipment. It is worth finding out early.

If your home already has solar panels or a battery backup system, home charging can become even more practical. Many owners find they can charge largely from their own solar generation during the day, which reduces both cost and dependence on the grid.

Workplace charging: the underrated option

After home, the workplace is where many owners find their second most reliable charging opportunity. The logic is the same as home charging. The car is already parked. It sits for hours while you work. That time can be used.

Workplace charging tends to be slower, similar to home AC charging, but that is fine for a full day of parking. By the time you leave, the battery is topped up. Not every employer offers this yet.

Some do, either as a staff benefit or through a shared charging bay. If yours does, it is worth factoring into your decision. If yours doesn't, it may be worth raising the conversation.

Destination charging: useful when it fits naturally

Some places you visit regularly may already have chargers available.

Shopping centres are the most common example in South Africa. Several major malls now have charging bays in their parking areas. If you spend an hour or two there each week, that time can add meaningful range.

The same applies to gyms, churches, schools, or anywhere else the car sits for a predictable stretch of time. Destination charging works best when it fits into something you are already doing. It is less useful as a dedicated trip.

Public fast charging: for the road, not the routine

Public DC fast chargers are the ones you see on highways and at fuel stops along major routes. They charge quickly. A stop of 20 to 40 minutes can add a significant amount of range, depending on the car and the charger.

They are genuinely useful for long-distance travel. Joburg to Durban, Cape Town to the Garden Route, or any trip that takes you beyond your comfortable daily range.

They are less suited to everyday use. The cost per kilowatt-hour is generally higher than home charging. The chargers can be occupied. They sometimes require specific apps or payment methods. And in South Africa, reliability is still improving. Public fast charging is a valuable part of the picture. It is just not the foundation.

How your living situation shapes everything

South Africa has a wide range of living situations, and they affect EV ownership in different ways.

A freestanding home with a garage is the most straightforward setup. Home charging is easy to arrange, and the car is protected overnight.

An estate or security complex can work well, but it depends on the rules and the available infrastructure. Some estates have already installed shared charging points. Others are still working through the process.

A flat or apartment is more challenging. If there is no dedicated parking with power access, home charging may not be possible. In that case, workplace charging or nearby destination chargers become more important.

A home with solar adds another layer of flexibility. Charging during the day from your own generation is increasingly common and can make the economics of EV ownership even more attractive.

The pattern that makes ownership easy

When you look at how most South African EV owners charge, a pattern emerges.

Home charging does the heavy lifting. Workplace charging fills in the gaps. Destination charging adds a little extra when it fits naturally. Public fast charging handles the long trips.

That combination, working together, is what makes daily EV ownership feel unremarkable in the best possible way. The car charges while life happens. You stop thinking about it.

What to check before you commit

If you are still considering an EV, it is worth mapping out your own charging picture before you buy.

Think through where the car will park at home, whether your workplace has or could have charging, which destinations you visit regularly, and how often you travel long distances. That map will tell you whether EV ownership is likely to feel easy or whether it will require more planning than you are comfortable with.

In the next article, we take a closer look at public charging in South Africa — what the networks look like, what to expect from a real charging stop, and how to plan so that a slow or unavailable charger never catches you off guard.

items.title: Jetour expands T-Series range with plug-in hybrid models
items.summary: Jetour South Africa has launched two plug-in hybrid variants of its T-Series SUV range, marking the brand's first entry into electrified powertrains since arriving in the country in September 2024. The T1 i-DM and T2…
items.content_html:
Jetour South Africa has launched two plug-in hybrid variants of its T-Series SUV range, marking the brand's first entry into electrified powertrains since arriving in the country in September 2024.

The T1 i-DM and T2 i-DM were unveiled at an event held at the SunBet Arena at Time Square in Pretoria. Both models are available immediately through the brand's network of more than 55 dealerships nationwide, priced from R689 900 for the T1 i-DM and R779 900 for the T2 i-DM.

Jetour's South African operation has grown rapidly since its launch, with monthly sales rising from around 400 units in 2024 to more than 1 700 units currently. The T-Series alone has recorded more than 4 587 sales within six months of its introduction, providing a strong foundation for the PHEV expansion.

At the core of the i-DM range is a plug-in hybrid powertrain producing a combined output of up to 265 kW. The system is designed to support both urban commuting and long-distance travel, with a combined driving range of over 1 200 km.

Pure electric driving is available for everyday use, while DC fast charging enables a 30 to 80% recharge in under 30 minutes. Fuel consumption is rated from as low as 1.1 litres per 100 km.

An intelligent energy management system monitors and optimises performance and efficiency across driving conditions. Nic Campbell, Vice President of Jetour South Africa, said the PHEV models address both environmental and practical considerations.

"In a market where energy costs continue to fluctuate, PHEVs offer real practical benefit by lowering fuel consumption and helping users reduce their day-to-day running costs," he said.

The i-DM models retain the boxy, rugged styling of the existing T-Series, with signature lighting elements, premium wheel designs and a panoramic glass sunroof. Inside, the cabin features a dual-screen display setup, wireless charging, ambient lighting and an advanced infotainment system.

items.title: Geely introduces E2 hatchback
items.summary: Geely Auto South Africa has introduced the E2, a compact all-electric five-door hatchback aimed at making electric mobility more attainable for everyday urban drivers. The model arrives in two specification grades, Aspire and Apex, priced…
items.content_html:
Geely Auto South Africa has introduced the E2, a compact all-electric five-door hatchback aimed at making electric mobility more attainable for everyday urban drivers. The model arrives in two specification grades, Aspire and Apex, priced at R339 900 and R389 900 respectively.

The E2 is built on a dedicated electric vehicle platform that enables a flat cabin floor, improved weight distribution and a long wheelbase relative to its overall footprint. At 4 135 mm in length with a 2 650 mm wheelbase, the car is designed to balance compact exterior dimensions with a practical interior.

Power comes from an 85 kW rear-wheel-drive electric motor producing 150 Nm of torque, paired with a 39.4 kWh lithium iron phosphate battery. Geely claims a WLTP range of up to 325 km and an energy consumption figure of 15.2 kWh per 100 km. The sprint from 0 to 100 km/h takes 11.5 seconds, with a top speed of 130 km/h.

Charging options include AC top-up from 10 to 100% in approximately 6.5 hours, and DC fast charging from 30 to 80% in around 25 minutes at up to 70 kW. The E2 also supports Vehicle-to-Load (V2L) functionality, allowing it to power external devices, and includes a battery pre-heating feature.

Inside, both variants feature a dual-display setup comprising a 14.6-inch infotainment touchscreen and an 8.8-inch digital instrument cluster. The boot offers 375 litres of space, expandable to 1 320 litres, with an additional 70-litre front trunk. More than 30 storage compartments are distributed throughout the cabin.

The Aspire grade covers the fundamentals with fabric seats, manual air conditioning, a rear camera, keyless entry and a four-speaker audio system. The Apex adds vegan leather upholstery, a power-adjustable driver's seat, wireless charging, ambient lighting, a six-speaker audio system and a 360-degree panoramic camera with transparent chassis view.

On the safety front, all models include ABS, ESC, six airbags, traction control, hill-start assist and tyre pressure monitoring. The Apex extends this with a full suite of advanced driver assistance systems, including adaptive cruise control, automatic emergency braking, lane departure warning and forward collision warning.

Connectivity across the range is handled by Geely's Flyme Auto Smart System, with Apple CarPlay, Android Auto, Bluetooth and 4G as standard.

Ownership benefits include a four-year or 150 000 km vehicle warranty, an eight-year or 200 000 km battery warranty, a three-year or 200 000 km service plan, and five-year unlimited kilometre roadside assistance. A wallbox home charger is included, subject to terms and conditions via Geely Finance.

Geely currently operates 32 dealerships in South Africa and has indicated plans to grow that network to 40 by the end of 2026.
